edges.evidence_quote: During the Prohibition era, a gang of Detroit mafiosi known as the River Gang smuggled Canadian beer and liquor into the United States from Ontario and controlled most of the bootlegging market in Southeast Michigan.
edges.context: The River Gang was a predecessor group that later evolved into the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: Utilizing its close links with the Sicilian Mafia, the Partnership became a major distributor of heroin in North America after World War II.
edges.context: The Detroit Partnership cooperated with the Sicilian Mafia to distribute heroin.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Badalamenti Mafia clan
edges.context: The Badalamenti Mafia clan is listed as an ally of the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Bufalino crime family
edges.context: The Bufalino crime family is listed as an ally of the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: Between 1912 and 1913, the Gianola brothers took over the Wyandotte area rackets gang starting a feud with Wyandotte Mafia boss Vito Adamo.
edges.context: The Gianola brothers feuded with the Adamo gang over control of rackets.

edges.evidence_quote: In late 1918, Vitale broke away from the Gianola gang and established himself independently, aligning himself with the remnants of the Bosco group. What became known as the 'Gianola-Vitale War' emerged as one of the bloodiest battles in the history of the Detroit Mafia.
edges.context: The Gianola brothers and the Vitale gang fought a war for control of Detroit's Italian underworld.

edges.evidence_quote: Catalanotte formed a strong alliance with Wyandotte area beer baron, Joseph Tocco and Hamtramck Mafia leader Chester La Mare. This faction became known as the Westside Mob. ... Meli reorganized the group under the 'Eastside Mob' flag.
edges.context: The Westside Mob and Eastside Mob were two factions that emerged after the Gianola-Vitale war, but the text does not state a relationship between them; they are separate factions under Catalanotte and Meli respectively.

edges.evidence_quote: Catalanotte organized the remaining Mafia factions under a liquor combine that became known as the 'Pascuzzi Combine'.
edges.context: The Westside Mob was part of the Pascuzzi Combine organized by Catalanotte.

edges.evidence_quote: Catalanotte organized the remaining Mafia factions under a liquor combine that became known as the 'Pascuzzi Combine'.
edges.context: The Eastside Mob was part of the Pascuzzi Combine organized by Catalanotte.

edges.evidence_quote: The Pascuzzi Combine created a unified and cohesive criminal organization that controlled liquor smuggling, bootlegging, gambling, prostitution, narcotics and other rackets and was the forerunner of the Detroit Partnership under the future Zerilli-Tocco regime.
edges.context: The Pascuzzi Combine was the forerunner of the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: Leaders of the Eastside Mob became the originators of what was to become Detroit's modern-day Mafia organization.
edges.context: The East Side Gang evolved into the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: Milazzo's Mafia group and the Eastside Gang maintained alliances with other mob factions, including the River Gang, led by brothers Thomas and Peter Licavoli who shared the local bootlegging rackets and various underworld activities with each other.
edges.context: The River Gang shared bootlegging rackets and underworld activities with the East Side Gang, which became the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: The Fish Market Murders developed into the major gang conflict between the Eastside and Westside Mobs, which would later be known as the Crosstown Mafia War.
edges.context: The East Side Gang and Westside Mob fought in the Crosstown Mafia War.

edges.evidence_quote: Gaspar Milazzo and his longtime ally, Buffalo Mafia boss Stefano Magaddino had secretly, but willingly supported their allies in New York and Chicago as they opposed Joe Masseria and his supporters.
edges.context: Milazzo and Magaddino were Castellammarese leaders who supported each other's families.

edges.evidence_quote: Both men openly supported their allies, including Chicago Mafia boss Giuseppe Aiello, who was being opposed by a powerful Masseria ally in Chicago, Al Capone.
edges.context: Castellammarese leaders supported Chicago Mafia boss Giuseppe Aiello against Al Capone.

edges.evidence_quote: LaMare began to make plans to eliminate the leaders of the Eastside gang... with the support of Joe Masseria in New York.
edges.context: Joe Masseria, a New York boss, supported the Westside Mob against the East Side Gang.

edges.evidence_quote: the Detroit Partnership was officially recognized within the Italian-American underworld's new organizational structure.
edges.context: The Detroit Partnership was recognized by The Commission as part of La Cosa Nostra.

edges.evidence_quote: After a 1956 Commission meeting, the crime families of Philadelphia, headed by Angelo Bruno, and Detroit, headed by Zerilli, were added to The Commission.
edges.context: Detroit Partnership was added as a member of The Commission in 1956.

edges.evidence_quote: As a commission member Zerilli acted as a senior L.C.N. leader alongside the five New York City bosses and other top Mafia bosses from around the country at the time that included Stefano Magaddino of Buffalo, Sam Giancana of Chicago, Angelo Bruno of Philadelphia, Santo Trafficante Jr. of Tampa, Carlos Marcello of New Orleans and Raymond Patriarca Sr. of New England.
edges.context: Detroit boss Zerilli served alongside Philadelphia boss Angelo Bruno on The Commission.

edges.evidence_quote: As a commission member Zerilli acted as a senior L.C.N. leader alongside the five New York City bosses and other top Mafia bosses from around the country at the time that included Stefano Magaddino of Buffalo, Sam Giancana of Chicago, Angelo Bruno of Philadelphia, Santo Trafficante Jr. of Tampa, Carlos Marcello of New Orleans and Raymond Patriarca Sr. of New England.
edges.context: Detroit boss Zerilli served alongside Buffalo boss Stefano Magaddino on The Commission.

edges.context: Detroit boss Zerilli served alongside Chicago boss Sam Giancana on The Commission.

edges.context: Detroit boss Zerilli served alongside Tampa boss Santo Trafficante Jr. on The Commission.

edges.context: Detroit boss Zerilli served alongside New Orleans boss Carlos Marcello on The Commission.

edges.evidence_quote: As a commission member Zerilli acted as a senior L.C.N. leader alongside the five New York City bosses and other top Mafia bosses from around the country at the time that included Stefano Magaddino of Buffalo, Sam Giancana of Chicago, Angelo Bruno of Philadelphia, Santo Trafficante Jr. of Tampa, Carlos Marcello of New Orleans and Raymond Patriarca Sr. of New England.
edges.context: Detroit boss Zerilli served alongside New England boss Raymond Patriarca Sr. on The Commission.

edges.evidence_quote: The 'Partinico faction' was a group of Detroit Mafia leaders that included Raffaele Quasarano and deported Sicilian-American mafiosi, Francesco 'Frankie Fingers' Coppola.
edges.context: The Partinico faction is a sub-group within the Detroit Partnership.

edges.evidence_quote: LaPiana was initially an associate in the Elmwood Park crew of the Chicago Outfit, where he was a protégé of John DiFronzo.
edges.context: Anthony LaPiana, a member of the Detroit Partnership, was previously an associate in the Chicago Outfit.
